The Associated Press also reports that President Trump is pressing Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz by Monday, while Iran has shown no clear sign of backing down. In practical terms, that means the market will begin this week with the same basic concern it had at the end of last week: even if the worst-case supply scenario does not happen, the threat alone is enough to keep oil and inflation fears elevated.

That is why the energy story remains the central economic story.

On Thursday, before the Good Friday closure, U.S. benchmark crude jumped to 111 dollars and 54 cents a barrel, while Brent closed above 109. Futures trading on Friday was quiet because the cash market was closed, but the message was still obvious. Investors are trying to figure out whether this is a panic spike that fades or the early stage of a more durable inflation shock. The distinction matters. If oil backs off quickly, then this can remain mostly a confidence story. If oil stays elevated into next week, then it starts feeding much more directly into transportation, shipping, household budgets, and the bond market's view of what the Federal Reserve can realistically do.

And that takes us directly into the March jobs report, which is the second major force shaping the week ahead.

According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, U.S. employers added 178,000 jobs in March, and the unemployment rate held near a relatively low 4.3 percent. Health care, construction, and transportation and warehousing all added jobs, while federal employment continued to drift lower. Average hourly earnings rose 0.2 percent for the month and 3.5 percent from a year earlier. That is a better picture than many analysts expected after February's weak showing.

The simplest way to read the report is that the labor market still has more resilience than the broader mood suggests. Employers are hiring selectively, not aggressively, but they are still hiring. The more complicated read is that this kind of report does not make the Fed's job easier when oil is already pushing in the wrong direction. A soft jobs market would have strengthened the argument for lower rates sooner. A steady jobs market, combined with elevated energy prices, tends to keep policymakers cautious.

That is exactly why Friday's market setup looked so conflicted.

Because U.S. stock markets were closed for Good Friday, investors did not get a full cash-session reaction. But AP reported that Friday morning futures slipped modestly after the jobs data, with S and P futures down 0.3 percent, Dow futures down 0.2 percent, and Nasdaq futures down 0.4 percent. That is not a crash signal. It is a hesitation signal. Markets are basically saying the same thing many households are saying: it is good that the economy still has momentum, but momentum is less comforting when it may also keep borrowing costs high.

So the Monday open will matter, not because it decides everything, but because it tells us which story investors want to emphasize first. If traders focus on jobs resilience, equities could stabilize. If they focus on oil and inflation risk, then last week's pressure can quickly carry forward.

Housing sits right in the middle of that tug-of-war.

Freddie Mac's latest weekly survey showed the average 30-year fixed mortgage rate at 6.46 percent, and Mortgage News Daily's daily index moved from 6.41 percent on April 2 to 6.45 percent on April 3. That is a useful summary of where the mortgage market stands right now. Rates are no longer at the late-March peak, but they are still plainly too high to feel comfortable for much of the market. There is some day-to-day improvement, then some backsliding, and not enough relief to change the affordability picture in a meaningful way.

The Mortgage Bankers Association's latest weekly survey also showed how sensitive this market still is. Mortgage applications fell 10.5 percent in the latest release, refinance activity dropped 15 percent from the prior week, and purchase applications slipped 5 percent. MBA's chief economist pointed directly at higher-for-longer oil prices, elevated Treasury yields, and affordability pressure. That is the important combination. Rates alone are not the whole problem. Prices are still high, inventory is uneven, and every new geopolitical shock makes it harder for buyers to believe financing conditions are about to improve.

In plain English, housing is not frozen, but it is still strained. People who need to move are moving. People who can wait are still waiting. And first-time buyers are absorbing most of the pain.

That national pattern is showing up very clearly in Central Ohio.

Axios Columbus reported that home sales in Central Ohio are shifting away from Franklin County and toward surrounding counties, a sign that affordability is changing where buyers are willing or able to look. Closings in Franklin County were down from a year earlier, while surrounding counties such as Delaware, Fairfield, Licking, and Madison posted gains. At the same time, homes are taking longer to sell than they did during the frenzy years, even though selective bidding wars are still starting to reappear.

That is a more balanced market than the pandemic-era rush, but it is not a cheap or easy one. It is a market where buyers are broadening their search radius because they have to, not because they suddenly prefer longer drives or more dispersed growth.

And the reason this is likely to remain a live local story through next week and beyond is that Columbus is still growing faster than much of the country. New census estimates highlighted by Axios show the Columbus metro added more than 21,000 residents from 2024 to 2025, nearly 1 percent growth and about double the national pace. The faster-growing ring counties are especially important because they reinforce the housing spillover story. When Franklin County tightens on price, demand does not disappear. It shifts outward.

That means the local housing conversation is no longer just about mortgage rates. It is also about infrastructure, commute tolerance, school districts, land use, and whether the region can keep adding supply fast enough in the places people can still afford.

Now step back to the broader market lens for a moment, because the same logic applies nationally.

The United States still has growth in the system. You can see it in payrolls. You can see it in population growth corridors like Central Ohio. You can see it in the willingness of large companies to keep spending on technology and infrastructure. But the environment around that growth is getting more expensive again. Higher oil makes everything feel heavier. It raises the cost of transportation. It complicates the inflation outlook. It nudges bond yields. It makes a monthly mortgage payment feel even less manageable. And it puts pressure on consumer confidence, even before all of the economic data has time to react.

That is why this weekend does not feel like a clean continuation of Friday's strong jobs headline. It feels like a collision between resilience and cost pressure.

Let's turn to technology, because the AI story also says something useful about the economy right now.

Microsoft announced three new in-house MAI models this past week, including a transcription model, a voice model, and an image model, all now available through Microsoft Foundry, with some also offered in MAI Playground. That matters because Microsoft is signaling, more clearly than before, that it does not want to sit only in the role of cloud host, enterprise distributor, or close partner to outside frontier labs. It wants more direct ownership of the model layer as well.

This is not just a product launch story. It is a platform-control story.

The AI market is maturing into a much more practical competition over who can offer reliable tools, usable pricing, enterprise integration, and enough compute to keep customers from redesigning workflows every quarter. Benchmark bragging still matters, but less than it did. Buyers increasingly care about whether a vendor can actually ship, support, and scale.

Microsoft's message is that it intends to compete on all of that at once. That reinforces the continuity thread we've been tracking: the AI race is moving away from pure model theater and toward a stack battle over infrastructure, distribution, and cost discipline.

Central Ohio has a stake in that too, even when the headlines are national. Regions that continue attracting logistics, manufacturing, warehousing, and data infrastructure will keep seeing the downstream effects in land use, utility demand, and housing. So when we talk about AI and compute at the national level, part of the local question is what kind of growth this region wants to absorb and how quickly it can build around it.

Now let's bring in the practical snapshot for today and tomorrow.

The National Weather Service outlook for Columbus points to a cooler turn after yesterday's warmth. Sunday looks mostly cloudy with a chance of showers and a high around the upper 50s, followed by a colder Sunday night and then a sunnier Monday with a high near the low 50s. So the headline is not severe weather this morning. It is a temperature reset. If Saturday felt like early summer, today is Ohio reminding everyone that it is still spring.

Before we close, here's the thread tying this morning together.

The economy did not lose its footing over the weekend. But the cost of keeping its footing looks higher than it did a week ago.

The labor market is still producing jobs. Mortgage rates are high and twitchy rather than collapsing lower. Oil remains the story with the most power to change everything else quickly. Markets did not panic on Friday, but they also did not get reassuring evidence that inflation pressure is about to disappear. Locally, Columbus keeps adding people and demand, but that strength is now expressing itself less as easy optimism and more as visible strain on affordability and geography.

So the thing to watch first this week is not just whether stocks go up or down on Monday morning. It is whether the dominant conversation becomes resilience or inflation shock. If crude cools and the Strait of Hormuz risk eases, then the jobs report could become the more important takeaway and markets may regain some confidence. If the war escalates again or oil stays pinned near current levels, then stronger hiring may be treated less as good news and more as one more reason the Fed cannot offer relief soon.

That same framing applies to homebuyers, business owners, and local planners. A resilient economy is good. A resilient economy with expensive energy and expensive financing is much harder to live in. That is the tension defining this moment.

For now, the United States still looks more resilient than weak. But the margin for error is narrowing. And that is why this next week matters.

That's your Morning Brief for Sunday, April 5th. Have a great day.

item.title: Morning Brief — Jobs Hold Up, Oil Stays Hot, and Spring Housing Gets Complicated
item.description: A stronger than expected jobs report steadied recession fears, Iran kept oil elevated, mortgage pressure lingered, Microsoft pushed harder into AI, and Central Ohio kept growing into its housing squeeze.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Saturday, April 4th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief.

This morning the story is not that the economy looks healthy or unhealthy in any simple way. The story is that several signals are pointing in different directions at once, and that makes this a much more complicated moment than any one headline suggests. The labor market just turned in a better than expected report. Oil is still elevated because of the war with Iran and the ongoing risk around the Strait of Hormuz. Mortgage rates remain high enough to frustrate buyers even as daily rates have eased a bit from last week's peak. And in technology, the AI race keeps broadening from model quality into a much more concrete fight over infrastructure, pricing, and who can actually deliver usable products at scale.

In other words, the economy still has momentum, but it is carrying more drag than it wants.

Let's start with the broad market picture.

Because Friday was Good Friday, the stock market itself was closed, so the cleanest snapshot comes from Thursday's close and Friday's futures trading. On Thursday, the S and P 500 finished at 6,582.69, the Nasdaq at 21,879.18, and the Dow at 46,504.67. Then on Friday morning, futures slipped modestly after the March jobs report came in stronger than expected. That combination tells you a lot about where investors are mentally right now. They are not trading a clean recession scare. They are trading a market that still sees resilience underneath the economy, but also understands that resilience can keep interest rates higher for longer, especially if energy costs stay under pressure.

And energy is still the main stress point. U.S. benchmark crude ended Thursday at 111 dollars and 54 cents a barrel, with Brent above 109. Those are not small moves. They reflect continuing concern that the war with Iran could drag on and that any deeper disruption around the Strait of Hormuz would hit global supply chains, shipping costs, and inflation expectations almost immediately. The United States does not need to import much oil directly from that region for Americans to feel it. Oil is globally priced. If traders think the waterway is less secure, the cost signal moves everywhere.

That leads directly into the biggest piece of national economic news from the last 24 hours: the March jobs report.

The Labor Department says U.S. employers added 178,000 jobs in March, a clear rebound from February's loss of 133,000. The unemployment rate edged down to 4.3 percent from 4.4 percent. On the surface, that looks like a healthy reset after a weak prior month. And to be fair, it is a better report than a lot of analysts were bracing for. Health care did much of the heavy lifting. Construction also got a boost, helped by better weather. Wages rose at a pace that does not immediately scream inflation panic.

But there is an important second reading here. The labor force also shrank, which helped pull the unemployment rate lower. So this was not a perfect all-clear signal. It was more of a reminder that the labor market still has some underlying strength even while households and employers remain cautious. That matters for the Federal Reserve, because a jobs market that refuses to break does not automatically justify easier policy, especially when oil is making the inflation picture messier again.

So if you are trying to reconcile the headlines this morning, the answer is this: the economy is not rolling over, but it is also not moving in a way that gives policymakers much comfort. Stronger hiring is good news for workers. It is also the kind of news that can keep bond yields from falling as much as borrowers would like.

And that brings us to housing.

Freddie Mac's latest weekly survey put the average 30-year fixed mortgage rate at 6.46 percent, up from 6.38 percent the week before. That is the headline number. But there is a nuance worth keeping in mind. Mortgage News Daily's daily gauge showed top-tier 30-year rates around 6.41 percent on Thursday, which suggests the day-to-day market has eased a little from the worst levels we saw late in March. In plain English, rates are still high, but the market may be trying to stabilize rather than continue straight upward.

That would normally be modestly encouraging for the spring market, except affordability is still the bigger issue. Mortgage applications data showed purchase demand slipping again week over week, while refinancing remains far stronger than a year ago because homeowners are quick to react to even small rate improvements. For buyers, especially first-time buyers, the problem is that a slightly better rate does not fix the price of the house, the monthly payment, or the lack of inventory where people actually want to live.

That national pattern is showing up very clearly here in Central Ohio.

Axios Columbus reported this week that home sales in Central Ohio are shifting away from Franklin County and toward surrounding counties as prices keep rising and the region keeps growing. Closings in Franklin County were down from a year earlier, while sales rose in counties like Delaware, Fairfield, Licking, and Madison. Homes are also spending longer on the market than they did during the frenzy years, even as multiple-offer situations are beginning to reappear in some segments. That is a much healthier market than the pandemic extremes, but it is still not an easy one. Buyers are being pushed outward by cost, while sellers no longer get to assume that any listing will fly off the shelf instantly.

And the reason that suburban shift matters is bigger than one season's sales data. Central Ohio keeps adding people. New census estimates highlighted by Axios show the Columbus metro added more than 21,000 residents from 2024 to 2025, roughly twice the national growth rate. More than half of Ohio's total growth came from this region. So the local housing story is not just rates. It is rates meeting population growth, limited stock in the county core, and a region that is still reorganizing itself around where jobs, infrastructure, and development are actually landing.

The practical takeaway is straightforward. If mortgage rates stay in the mid-sixes and Central Ohio keeps outgrowing the rest of the state, the pressure does not disappear. It just redistributes. More demand spills into the ring counties. More commute tolerance becomes normal. And more of the housing conversation becomes about land use, infrastructure, schools, and transportation instead of just monthly rates.

Now back to Washington for another thread that still deserves attention even without a brand-new ruling overnight.

The birthright citizenship case remains one of the most important constitutional stories on the board. After this week's Supreme Court arguments, the administration still appears to face a difficult road. Multiple justices, including some conservatives, sounded reluctant to endorse the White House theory that a president can effectively narrow the Fourteenth Amendment's citizenship guarantee by executive action. The reason this continues to matter this weekend is that it is part of a broader test of executive reach. Markets care about that too, even when they are not saying so loudly. If courts show they are willing to put firmer limits on aggressive federal actions, then investors, employers, and state governments all have to rethink how durable certain policy moves really are.

That same question of durability is also hanging over trade policy. On Thursday, President Trump announced a new pharmaceutical tariff framework that could eventually reach 100 percent on some patented imported drugs if companies do not strike pricing deals with the administration and move more production into the United States. The tariffs would not hit immediately, and companies still have months to negotiate. But the message is clear: even after legal setbacks to some earlier tariff strategies, the White House is still looking for alternative routes to use trade pressure as industrial policy.

It is worth watching not just as a political story but as a cost story. Pharma, like autos, electronics, and industrial machinery, sits right at the intersection of supply chains, pricing power, and public sensitivity. If this becomes another area where businesses have to model tariff risk alongside energy risk, then the administration's economic posture stays more inflationary and more operationally unpredictable than many executives would prefer.

Let's turn to technology, where the last 24 hours brought another useful reminder that the AI race is becoming more crowded and more physical at the same time.

Microsoft announced three new in-house MAI models now available through Microsoft Foundry and the MAI Playground: a transcription model, a voice model, and an image model. On one level, this is a normal product launch. On another, it is Microsoft sending a very direct signal that it does not want to be seen only as the cloud home or commercial wrapper for someone else's models. It wants a larger share of the stack, from foundation model strategy to enterprise tooling to consumer-facing product integration.

And that matters because the competitive map in AI is shifting. Enterprises are no longer choosing only on benchmark headlines. They are choosing on availability, price, speed, interoperability, and whether a vendor looks like it can keep shipping. The more Microsoft can offer its own model family inside a broader platform, the more leverage it has in those enterprise conversations.

At the same time, the broader industry is still staring at the same bottleneck we have been talking about for days: compute. Even with giant capital plans and new partnerships, the capacity question has not gone away. That is why the most important AI story right now is not just who has the smartest demo. It is who can deliver dependable performance at scale without making customers wait, overpay, or rebuild their workflows every six months.

That AI infrastructure story also has a local angle. Central Ohio keeps benefiting from being a growth corridor for logistics, advanced manufacturing, and data infrastructure. But that growth is starting to generate more friction over energy use, tax incentives, and what communities actually get in return. That tension is not going away. If this region remains one of the country's most important expansion zones for tech-related infrastructure while also absorbing rapid population growth, local debates over power, water, roads, and land use are going to get sharper, not quieter.

One more note on Columbus before we get to the forecast. The local growth story increasingly feels like the lens through which other stories make more sense. Housing pressure, suburban sales shifts, data-center politics, school planning, and commute patterns all connect back to the same reality: Central Ohio is still growing faster than a lot of the country at a moment when many places are slowing down. That is a strength, but it also means this region has less room for policy drift and infrastructure lag than it used to.

And now the weather, because Ohio is about to do the full spring swing again.

The National Weather Service forecast for Columbus calls for a high near 80 today, with showers and thunderstorms likely this afternoon and tonight. Gusts could reach around 30 miles per hour, and some of the heavier rain will come inside thunderstorms. By Sunday, the pattern changes quickly. The high drops to around 51, with only a lingering morning shower chance before cooler and drier air moves in. Monday looks mostly sunny with a high in the upper 50s. So if you have outdoor plans today or tonight, keep an eye on the radar. And if you have been fooled into putting the jackets away, not so fast.

Before I let you go, here's the thread tying today's brief together.

The United States still has growth in the system. You can see it in payrolls. You can see it in Central Ohio's population gains. You can see it in the amount of capital still chasing AI infrastructure and housing demand. But growth right now is happening in a more expensive, less certain environment. Oil is high. Mortgage rates are high. Trade policy remains volatile. And even the most exciting technology sector in the economy is running into hard physical limits.

That is why the mood this weekend feels so mixed. The country is not acting like it is in immediate retreat. But it is also not operating with much margin for error. If oil cools, rates could settle and spring sentiment could improve quickly. If the Iran story worsens, that pressure will show up everywhere from gas stations to bond yields to homebuying decisions.

So the thing to watch next is whether the current resilience starts to absorb the pressure, or whether the pressure starts to eat into the resilience. That is the real contest as we head into next week.

That's your Morning Brief for Saturday, April 4th. Have a great weekend.

item.title: Morning Brief — Supreme Court Pressure, Iran Oil Shock, and a Mortgage Market on Edge
item.description: The Supreme Court appeared skeptical of Trump's birthright citizenship order as Iran tensions kept oil elevated, mortgage rates climbed again, and Columbus looked ahead to a stormy spring weekend.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Friday, April 3rd, 2026. This is The Morning Brief.

This morning the big picture is pretty clear: Washington is juggling constitutional pressure at home, military uncertainty abroad, and a market that is trying very hard to believe the worst may still be avoidable. The Supreme Court sounded skeptical of the administration's effort to rewrite birthright citizenship. At the same time, the Iran conflict is still driving oil, rates, and investor nerves. And here at home, that pressure is showing up in one of the most practical places possible: the mortgage market.

Let's get into it.

A quick look at the markets heading into the long weekend. Stocks finished Thursday in mixed but calmer fashion after a very volatile session. The S and P 500 closed at 6,582.69, up a tenth of a percent. The Nasdaq added about two tenths of a percent to close at 21,879.18. The Dow slipped 61 points to 46,504.67. That headline sounds tame, but it hides the real story: investors spent much of the day absorbing another spike in oil prices and another round of uncertainty around Iran and the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. crude settled above 111 dollars a barrel. Mortgage rates, meanwhile, are still elevated, with Freddie Mac's weekly survey showing the average 30-year fixed at 6.46 percent, up again from 6.38 last week.

Let's start at the Supreme Court.

On Wednesday, the justices heard arguments in the administration's attempt to narrow birthright citizenship, and the immediate read from the courtroom was not encouraging for the White House. Several justices, including conservatives, appeared openly skeptical of the idea that a president can reinterpret the Fourteenth Amendment's citizenship guarantee by executive order. Chief Justice John Roberts and Justice Brett Kavanaugh both pressed the administration on why long-settled American constitutional practice should suddenly be read differently now. President Trump attended the arguments in person, which by itself was historic. But the larger story was the legal tone inside the chamber: this did not look like a bench eager to deliver the administration a clean constitutional victory.

That does not mean the issue is over. It means the administration now appears to face an uphill path in a case that goes to the core of who counts as an American citizen at birth. For financial professionals and business owners, there is also a practical takeaway here. Immigration fights often land as political abstraction until courts begin signaling real limits on executive power. This week was one of those moments. When a court appears wary of aggressive executive reinterpretation, that matters beyond immigration. It affects how investors price legal risk across a whole range of federal actions.

Now stay with that thought, because the same broader theme is showing up in trade.

The tariff refund story remains one of the strangest economic aftershocks in the country right now. After the Supreme Court struck down the administration's use of emergency powers for broad tariff policy earlier this year, Customs has been racing to build the refund machinery. The latest filings show the system is progressing, but not smoothly. Officials say the refund platform is between roughly 60 and 85 percent complete, and once claims begin moving, some payments may still take up to 45 days to process. The bigger catch is that the first version of the system will not be able to handle every claim immediately. Reports yesterday indicated the launch version may initially process only about two-thirds of the affected requests.

That matters because we are not talking about a niche customs dispute. We are talking about roughly 166 billion dollars in tariff collections that the government now owes back, with interest, to importers. For sectors tied to construction, furnishings, logistics, and manufactured inputs, this remains a live liquidity story. If the portal launches on time in mid-April, some companies could see real balance sheet relief before the end of the month. If the rollout slips, the backlog and frustration will deepen quickly. So the watch item here remains the same, but more urgent now: not whether refunds are coming, but whether the system can actually handle the volume.

The other major force driving markets this morning is still Iran.

The White House's public messaging continues to swing between escalation and diplomacy, and markets are reacting to every version of that story. Axios reported yesterday that U.S. and Iranian officials are discussing a possible ceasefire framework tied to reopening the Strait of Hormuz. At the same time, the administration's rhetoric has remained aggressive, and that combination is exactly why oil is staying elevated. Traders are not pricing a clean peace. They are pricing uncertainty around whether the world's most important energy chokepoint stays partially constrained, reopens under a monitored framework, or becomes the center of an even broader confrontation.

For households, the most direct effect is gasoline. For businesses, it is broader than that. Elevated oil bleeds into freight, plastics, construction materials, airline costs, food transport, and inflation expectations. And that is where it reconnects to housing finance. When bond markets start worrying that an oil shock could keep inflation hotter for longer, Treasury yields stay firm. And when Treasury yields stay firm, mortgage rates stop giving buyers the relief they were hoping for this spring.

That brings us to home lending.

Freddie Mac's latest survey put the 30-year fixed mortgage at 6.46 percent this week. That is not an emergency level by historic standards, but it is exactly the wrong direction for a market that needed lower rates to support the spring buying season. The Wall Street Journal also reported that purchase mortgage applications slipped another 3 percent, while home touring activity is lagging last year's pace. Put simply: buyers are still out there, but they are more cautious, more payment-sensitive, and more likely to pause when rates jump quickly.

This is where the national macro story becomes kitchen-table math. A move of a few tenths on mortgage rates may sound minor on television. On an actual monthly payment, it is not minor at all. Every time rates move up just enough to change affordability, borrowers lose options. Some stretch into smaller homes. Some move farther out. Some sit out entirely. And for lenders, agents, and builders, this kind of choppy environment is difficult because demand does not disappear cleanly. It hesitates. It stalls. It becomes harder to read.

The next signal to watch is whether the rate spike tied to the Iran conflict starts to ease next week, or whether it hardens into a more durable spring headwind. If oil cools and bond yields relax, this market could stabilize. If geopolitical stress keeps feeding inflation fears, another week of weak application data would not be surprising.

Now to AI and technology.

The theme there this week is that the competitive race is no longer just about model quality. It is about distribution, enterprise adoption, and raw compute capacity. Microsoft announced yesterday that it is putting three in-house MAI models into Foundry and its MAI Playground: a transcription model, a voice model, and an image model. That is notable not just because the products are new, but because it shows Microsoft continuing to diversify beyond being primarily the infrastructure and distribution partner for OpenAI. The company is making a more explicit case that it wants its own stack in market, and soon.

At the same time, OpenAI's own finance leadership is acknowledging a constraint the whole industry has been trying to outrun: compute. Business Insider reported that OpenAI is passing on some opportunities because it still does not have enough computing capacity. That lines up with the broader picture we have been covering. Anthropic has been gaining enterprise ground. Microsoft is broadening its model portfolio. And the real bottleneck increasingly looks physical, not conceptual. Demand for AI is huge. The ability to serve that demand reliably and profitably is still limited.

Why does that matter to the rest of the economy? Because we are moving out of the era where AI headlines were mostly about demos and into the era where they are about vendor choice, infrastructure spending, and workflow replacement. If you run a company, the question is no longer whether AI matters. The question is which platform can actually support your use case at scale without pricing surprises, outages, or strategic drift.

Closer to home in Columbus and Central Ohio, the local story is still growth, but with a more civic and neighborhood-level texture than the national headlines. One item that stood out yesterday: the movement that saved the Columbus Crew is now echoing well beyond Ohio. Axios Columbus reported that a newly introduced federal proposal called the Home Team Act was inspired in part by the Save The Crew fight and Ohio's own law requiring owners of publicly supported teams to give communities a chance to buy before relocating. Even if the bill faces long odds in Congress, it is another sign that Columbus keeps exporting civic models in ways that are easy to underestimate from the inside.

There is also continued movement around local cultural and neighborhood investment. Columbus Underground reported that the Poindexter Village African American Museum now has a formal groundbreaking date set for May 6, with construction expected to start soon after. That matters because Poindexter Village is not just another museum project. It is tied to one of the most historically significant African American communities in Columbus and to a broader local effort to invest in places with real civic memory, not just new square footage.

And if you are simply planning your weekend, the weather is about to remind everyone that spring in Ohio is never linear. The National Weather Service forecast for Columbus calls for a high near 80 today, with showers and thunderstorms becoming more likely this afternoon. Saturday stays warm, near 80 again, with a stronger chance of showers and storms. Then Sunday turns sharply cooler, with the high dropping into the low 50s after the rain moves through. So if you have outside plans, especially on Saturday evening, keep an eye on the radar.

Before I let you go, here's the single thread connecting most of today's brief.

The country is in one of those moments when legal uncertainty, geopolitical uncertainty, and borrowing-cost uncertainty are all feeding one another. The Supreme Court is signaling limits on executive power. The tariff refund story is a live reminder that large policy swings can come back as operational and financial headaches. Iran is keeping oil and rates under pressure. And the housing market is absorbing the consequences in real time. Then on the technology side, the AI leaders are discovering that demand alone does not guarantee durable advantage if compute and distribution become the true choke points.

So the thing to watch next is not just whether one headline breaks through. It is whether several of these pressures begin resolving in the same direction. If ceasefire diplomacy gains traction, oil could cool, rates could soften, and mortgage sentiment could improve fairly quickly. If not, the market may have to keep grinding through a much more expensive spring than anyone wanted.

That's your Morning Brief for Friday, April 3rd. Have a great weekend. I'll see you Monday.

item.title: Morning Brief — Liberation Day Anniversary: $166B Refunds Due, SCOTUS Skeptical of Trump
item.description: Today marks one year since Trump's Liberation Day tariffs — the Supreme Court struck them down and $166 billion in refunds are weeks away. Conservative justices questioned birthright citizenship; Iran ceasefire talks lifted stocks.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Thursday, April 2nd, 2026. This is The Morning Brief.

One year ago today, in the White House Rose Garden, President Trump signed the executive order he called Liberation Day — launching the most sweeping tariff regime in nearly a hundred years. Today we have a one-year reckoning. And separately, the Supreme Court heard arguments yesterday on birthright citizenship, with the president's own appointees appearing to push back hard on his case.

Let's get into it.

A quick look at the markets. The S&P 500 closed Wednesday at sixty-five seventy-five, up about seven tenths of a percent. The Dow added roughly two hundred twenty-four points to close near forty-six thousand five sixty-six, up just under half a percent. The Nasdaq led the session with a gain of just over one percent, closing at twenty-one thousand eight forty-one. It was the best session of the week, driven largely by reports that the United States and Iran are in active ceasefire discussions. The ten-year Treasury yield sits at four point three eight percent this morning, up slightly from yesterday's close. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is tracking at roughly six point three five percent according to Optimal Blue — down about five basis points from the prior session.

Let's start with the story that is both the anniversary of the day and the biggest economic policy reckoning in recent memory. One year ago today — April 2nd, 2025 — President Trump signed the executive order imposing sweeping reciprocal tariffs on imports from nearly every trading partner. At their peak, those tariffs pushed the average U.S. effective import tax rate above twenty percent, with duties on Chinese goods briefly reaching one hundred forty-five percent — essentially halting trade with the country that supplies roughly fifteen percent of American imports.

One year on, the results are in on the administration's stated goals — and they are not what the White House promised. The trade deficit, the central target of the policy, did not shrink. It grew. The Bureau of Economic Analysis reported last month that the U.S. goods deficit hit an all-time high in 2025, rising about two percent to one point two four trillion dollars. Manufacturing employment fell by eighty-nine thousand jobs between April 2025 and February of this year. And in February, the Supreme Court struck down the tariffs entirely, ruling that the administration's use of emergency powers under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act was not a legal basis for broad tariff authority.

Now the government is preparing the largest tariff refund in American history. Customs officials confirmed a plan to return roughly one hundred sixty-six billion dollars to approximately three hundred thirty thousand importers — plus interest. The first phase of the refund portal is expected to launch by mid-April. That timeline faces real logistics challenges — there are an estimated fifty-three million import entries subject to review — but officials say they're on track. For businesses that have been carrying that tariff cost on their books for the past year, the refund is significant, though analysts expect processing delays and disputes will stretch actual disbursements well into the second half of the year.

Meanwhile, at the Supreme Court yesterday, the administration was defending a different executive order on entirely different constitutional grounds — its attempt to end automatic birthright citizenship for children born to undocumented immigrants. The case is called Trump versus Barbara, and it directly challenges the Fourteenth Amendment's citizenship clause, which has guaranteed citizenship for nearly anyone born on American soil since 1868.

President Trump did something that has not happened in modern times: he attended oral arguments himself, sitting for about ninety minutes in the chamber. But the reception from the bench was notably cool, including from justices he appointed. Amy Coney Barrett and Neil Gorsuch both pressed hard on the administration's legal theory. Chief Justice Roberts brushed back the government's argument that we're in a new world since the Fourteenth Amendment was ratified, stating simply: "It's the same Constitution." NPR and multiple legal reporters in the room described a majority of justices as appearing ready to rule against the administration. A decision is expected before the end of June.

On the geopolitical front — day thirty-three of the U.S.-Israel military operation that began February twenty-eighth with surprise airstrikes and the killing of Supreme Leader Khamenei. The conflict has been the primary driver of oil price volatility and a weight on global markets for a month. Yesterday brought the first meaningful public signal of potential de-escalation. President Trump said Iran's president had reached out requesting a ceasefire. Iran officially denied that characterization, with the Foreign Minister telling Al Jazeera that Tehran is not seeking a ceasefire but wants to end the war permanently. Behind the scenes, though, three U.S. officials told Axios that discussions are actively underway — focused on a deal in which Iran reopens the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for a guaranteed halt to hostilities. Markets took that as a clear positive, hence Wednesday's broad rally. Whether it holds depends entirely on bridging a significant gap between what each side claims publicly and what they may be willing to accept privately.

Turning to home lending — the mortgage market is entering April under real pressure, and the Iran conflict's effect on rates is a significant part of that story.

The Mortgage Bankers Association reported this week that mortgage applications fell another ten point four percent in the most recent weekly period — the third consecutive drop of more than ten percent in a row. Refinance applications are down forty percent just since the start of March. Purchase applications fell three percent over the same stretch. The primary driver has been rate volatility: the thirty-year fixed spiked forty-eight basis points through March as the Iran conflict pushed oil higher and re-ignited inflation concerns among bond investors.

This week's modest pullback — to roughly six point three five percent from above six point four — has not been enough to get borrowers moving again. For context, the thirty-year rate was hovering closer to six percent back in January, before the Iran premium started getting priced in. For originators, that spread represents a demand problem that is effectively frozen most of the quarter.

On the regulatory side, the CFPB continues reshaping the enforcement landscape in ways that have real consequences for lenders. The bureau has reduced supervisory exams by roughly fifty percent and cut the share of oversight aimed at non-bank lenders from sixty to thirty percent — a significant retreat from the footprint of the prior administration. The practical result is a growing patchwork: California and several other states have moved aggressively to fill the gap with their own consumer protections, meaning national lenders now face a fragmented compliance environment rather than a single federal standard. For any shop with meaningful volume in multiple states, that complexity is a cost line item that wasn't there two years ago.

There's also a longer-term dynamic worth tracking: as the tariff refunds flow back to importers, a meaningful portion is expected to hit construction and building materials supply chains. For high-growth housing markets like Columbus, any easing of materials costs could provide a modest boost to new-home construction — at a time when the region is running five to ten thousand units per year behind what population growth demands.

In artificial intelligence, the competitive dynamics between the two leading frontier labs are shifting faster than most enterprise buyers have internalized.

Bloomberg reported yesterday that institutional investors holding roughly six hundred million dollars in OpenAI secondary shares have been unable to find a single buyer. This is striking for a company valued at eight hundred fifty-two billion dollars following its most recent fundraising round. On the same secondary platforms where OpenAI shares sit unsold, buyers say they are sitting on roughly two billion dollars in dry powder looking for a home — and they want Anthropic.

The secondary market dynamics mirror what's happened in actual enterprise spend. Earlier this month, Axios reported that Anthropic now accounts for about sixty-five percent of combined enterprise spending across OpenAI and Anthropic — up from roughly ten percent just fourteen months ago. OpenAI's enterprise API market share has dropped from fifty percent in 2023 to about twenty-five percent today. On revenue, OpenAI is approaching twenty-five billion dollars annualized; Anthropic is approaching nineteen billion. The gap is closing fast. Anthropic's revenue has been growing at roughly ten times per year versus OpenAI's three times. Epoch AI projects that at those trajectories, Anthropic will surpass OpenAI in annualized revenue by mid-2026 — which, if you're listening this morning, is about three months from now.

The practical implication for financial services professionals: Anthropic derives roughly eighty-five percent of its revenue from enterprise customers, versus OpenAI's primarily consumer-driven model. That split reflects where real institutional adoption is actually happening — where teams are embedding AI into workflows rather than experimenting with it through a chat interface. If your organization has not revisited its AI vendor relationships recently, that competitive picture is moving fast enough to warrant a fresh look.

Closer to home — Columbus is looking at a pleasant high of seventy-seven degrees today, though some scattered showers may develop this afternoon and evening, with rain expected to linger into the weekend.

On the business side, Drees Homes announced this week it is expanding into the Columbus market, making Central Ohio its third Ohio foothold alongside Cincinnati and Cleveland. Drees cited Columbus's economic momentum as the draw — tech employment in the region is up twenty-two percent since 2023, with Intel, Google, and Microsoft all anchoring significant presences here. The expansion adds one more national builder to a market that has been undersupplied for years.

The Capitol Square Renaissance project continues moving through the pipeline. The six-hundred-million-dollar, ten-acre redevelopment in the heart of downtown will eventually bring more than a thousand new residential units, nine restaurants, and roughly two hundred thousand square feet of office space. It's one of several projects attempting to address what housing analysts describe as a persistent structural gap: the Greater Columbus region needs between fourteen and nineteen thousand new homes per year and has been delivering roughly eight to nine thousand annually for the past decade. Until supply catches up with demand in a meaningful way, upward pressure on home prices and rents in Central Ohio is going to continue.

And a quieter but welcome piece of news: Columbus Metro Parks opened its newest green space this week — a seventy-acre Great Southern Metro Park along South High Street, bringing meaningful parkland to a part of the city that has historically lacked it.

Before I let you go, one thing to watch by mid-month: the opening of the federal tariff refund portal. Customs officials have committed to a mid-April launch for the first phase, targeting an initial one hundred twenty billion dollars in disbursements to the highest-priority importers. Watch for whether that deadline holds — and watch the political dynamics around it, since the administration will essentially be writing checks acknowledging that its signature economic policy was unconstitutional. For businesses that paid those tariffs and have been waiting, the question is timing and process. For the economy more broadly, an injection of over a hundred and sixty billion dollars is a genuinely unusual fiscal event with implications for supply chains, capital allocation, and potentially even consumer prices heading into summer.

That's your Morning Brief for Thursday. Have a great day. I'll see you tomorrow.
